source.title: Multi-regional control of amygdalar dynamics reliably reflects fear memory age

confidence_rationale: Shows coordinated BLA-CA1 theta coupling as a reliable neural signature of fear memory age, with BLA gamma entrained by CA1 theta during remote memory recall. Provides evidence for ongoing CA1-BLA coordination during fear memory expression, supporting the circuit substrate for MECH-074d's remap_signal pathway. However the paper characterises ongoing oscillatory coupling during recall, not a discrete PE-triggered remap event; the mapping to MECH-074d's acute event-triggered remap_signal is indirect. Mixed because it supports the circuit but does not directly test the harm-PE trigger mechanism.
failure_signatures: If BLA gamma entrainment by CA1 theta is a readout of memory recall (not a trigger for memory update), then the oscillatory coupling does not support an active remap_signal.

If the CA1 theta that entrains BLA gamma is from different place cells than those that remap (i.e. recall cells vs remap cells are non-overlapping), the coupling evidence does not constrain the remap mechanism.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Remote (older) contextual fear memory recall is characterised by weaker but more rhythmic BLA gamma activity entrained by CA1 theta oscillations; multi-regional CA1 theta + BLA gamma features reliably classify memory age. The ACC also contributes theta entrainment of BLA during remote memory.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-074d requires BLA to receive harm-PE information and fire remap_signal back to hippocampus. Makino et al. show CA1 theta entrains BLA gamma during fear recall -- suggesting a CA1 → BLA oscillatory channel that could carry PE-related information. The theta-gamma coupling architecture supports the hypothesis that BLA is dynamically reading CA1 hippocampal state, providing a substrate for attribution gating.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This paper addresses ongoing recall oscillations, not acute PE-triggered remap events. The remap_signal in MECH-074d is a discrete event occurring on harm-PE spike; oscillatory coupling during recall is background coordination, not an interrupt signal. Also: remote vs recent distinction is not the same as PE-triggered vs PE-absent.
mapping.source_context: Freely behaving mice, longitudinal multisite electrophysiology (CA1, BLA, ACC), contextual fear conditioning, recent vs remote memory recall, LFP spectral analysis.

confidence_components.notes: Nat Commun 2024, McHugh lab, RIKEN. High source quality. Mapping fidelity lower because oscillatory coupling during recall is not the same as acute PE-triggered remap. Evidence is indirect.
